subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will explore probability – the mathematics of chance and likelihood. Every single day you meet events that might happen, that will surely happen, or that could never happen at all. When Amina wonders whether it will rain before she reaches the masjid, when Yusuf flips a bottle cap to decide who kicks off the football match, or when Halima guesses which colour goat will come home first, they are all thinking about probability without even naming it.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the standardized Cambridge Stage 4 workbook source rows for screen presentation.

outcomes: Use the language of chance – certain, likely, even chance, unlikely, impossible – to describe events.
Compare the likelihood of two or more different events and put them in order.
Place events accurately on a probability scale from impossible to certain.
List all the possible outcomes of a simple experiment such as flipping a coin or rolling a dice.
Collect and organise data using tally charts and simple tables.
Use data to work out likelihood and to make predictions about future events.
Design and carry out simple probability experiments and record the results carefully.
Judge whether a game or spinner is fair, using probability to explain your thinking.

concepts.title: The Language of Chance
concepts.explanation: Probability begins with words. Before we ever use numbers, we describe how likely an event is using five special words. Learn them carefully, because you will use them throughout the whole unit. Certain – the event will definitely happen. There is a 100% chance. Example: after Monday comes Tuesday.
concepts.example: Likely – the event will probably happen, but it is not guaranteed. Example: it will rain during the rainy season.

concepts.title: The Probability Scale
concepts.explanation: We can draw the ladder of chance as a line, and this line is called the probability scale. Picture a straight line. At the far left end we write Impossible. At the far right end we write Certain. Exactly in the middle we write Even chance. Between impossible and the middle we write Unlikely, and between the middle and certain we write Likely. Impossible — Unlikely — Even chance — Likely — Certain. That is the scale, running from left (no chance) to right (sure to happen). Every event in the world can be placed…
concepts.example: Let us place some events. ‘The sun will set this evening’ goes at the far right, on Certain. ‘A dog will speak English to you’ goes at the far left, on Impossible. ‘Rolling an even number on a dice’ goes right in the…

concepts.title: Listing Outcomes
concepts.explanation: An outcome is one possible result of an experiment. When we roll a dice, flip a coin, or pick a marble, each thing that could happen is an outcome. To think clearly about probability, we first list all the possible outcomes – carefully, so that none is missed and none is counted twice. When you flip a coin, there are exactly two possible outcomes: heads or tails. When you roll a normal dice, there are six possible outcomes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. When you pick a single card from a deck by colour, there are two…
concepts.example: Once we have listed the outcomes, we can find the probability of an event as a fraction. The rule is: probability = number of ways the event can happen ÷ total number of outcomes. For example, the probability of rolling…

concepts.title: Comparing Likelihoods
concepts.explanation: Often we need to decide which of two events is more likely. The secret is to compare their probabilities. Think of each probability as a fraction – how many favourable outcomes out of the total – and the larger fraction wins. Which is more likely: getting heads on a coin flip, or rolling a 6 on a dice? The coin gives heads with probability 1 out of 2. The dice gives a 6 with probability 1 out of 6. Since 1 out of 2 is greater than 1 out of 6, getting heads is more likely. You can feel this is right: there are…
concepts.example: Here is a helpful idea: the more possible outcomes there are, the less likely any single one becomes. A coin has just 2 outcomes, so each has a 1 in 2 chance. A dice has 6 outcomes, so each has a 1 in 6 chance. A…

concepts.title: Collecting and Using Data
concepts.explanation: So far we have worked out probability by thinking – this is called theoretical probability. But we can also discover likelihood by collecting data from the real world. Data is information we gather by watching and counting, and a neat way to collect it is with a tally chart. In a tally chart, each time something happens you draw a small mark. To make counting easy, you group the marks in fives: draw four straight marks, then cross them with a fifth mark to make a bundle of five. This means you can count quickly in…
concepts.example: Imagine Idris stands outside Nuur Academy and records the colours of cars that pass. After a while his tally chart shows: red 7, blue 5, silver 11, black 8, white 10, other 3. Adding these gives a total of 7 + 5 + 11 +…

concepts.title: Probability Experiments
concepts.explanation: A probability experiment is an activity where we test how likely different outcomes really are. By running experiments we can check whether our predictions match what actually happens. To design a good experiment, follow six clear steps: Make a prediction about the likelihood of each outcome.
concepts.example: Decide how many times to repeat the experiment.

methods.title: How to find a probability
methods.example: Describe the likelihood of each event using certain, likely, even chance, unlikely or impossible: (a) the sun will rise tomorrow; (b) rolling a 7 on a normal dice; (c) getting tails on a fair coin.
steps: List all the possible outcomes.
Count how many outcomes are favourable (the ones you want).
Count the total number of outcomes.
Write the probability as favourable out of total, for example 2 out of 6.

methods.title: How to compare two likelihoods
methods.example: Put these in order from least likely to most likely: rolling a 6 on a dice; the sun setting tonight; a fish climbing a tree.
steps: Work out each probability as a fraction (favourable out of total).
Compare the fractions – the larger one is more likely.
Remember: more possible outcomes means each single outcome is less likely.

methods.title: How to run a probability experiment
methods.example: A bag holds 3 red, 2 blue and 1 green marble. What is the probability of picking blue?
steps: Decide what to test and list the outcomes.
Write a prediction before you start.
Repeat many times and tally the results.
Compare the results with your prediction, and remember small samples wobble.

methods.title: Compare two likelihoods
methods.example: Which is more likely: drawing a red card from a deck, or drawing a king?
steps: Solution: Red cards = 26 out of 52 = 1/2.
Kings = 4 out of 52.
Since 26 is much greater than 4, drawing a red card is more likely.

methods.title: Use a tally chart
methods.example: A spinner with red, blue, green and yellow is spun 40 times: red 14, blue 8, green 9, yellow 9. If the spinner were fair, how many times would each colour be expected, and does the data suggest it is fair?
steps: Solution: A fair spinner would give each colour 40 ÷ 4 = 10 spins.
Red appeared 14 times, well above 10, while the others were near it.
This suggests the spinner may not be completely fair – red seems to come up more often than expected.

methods.title: Judge fairness of a game
methods.example: Player 1 wins if a dice shows 1, 2 or 3; Player 2 wins if it shows 4, 5 or 6. Is this game fair?
steps: Solution: Player 1 wins with 3 outcomes; Player 2 wins with 3 outcomes.
Both have a 3 out of 6 chance, which is 1/2 each.
The game is fair.

workedExamples.title: Choose the right chance word
workedExamples.prompt: Describe the likelihood of each event using certain, likely, even chance, unlikely or impossible: (a) the sun will rise tomorrow; (b) rolling a 7 on a normal dice; (c) getting tails on a fair coin.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) Certain – it happens every day. (b) Impossible – a dice only has 1 to 6. (c) Even chance – there are two equally likely results, heads or tails, so tails has a 1 in 2 chance.

workedExamples.title: Place events on the scale
workedExamples.prompt: Put these in order from least likely to most likely: rolling a 6 on a dice; the sun setting tonight; a fish climbing a tree.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: ‘A fish climbing a tree’ is impossible (least likely). ‘Rolling a 6’ is unlikely (1 in 6). ‘The sun setting tonight’ is certain (most likely). Order: fish climbing a tree, rolling a 6, the sun setting.

workedExamples.title: List outcomes and find a probability
workedExamples.prompt: A bag holds 3 red, 2 blue and 1 green marble. What is the probability of picking blue?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total marbles = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6. Blue marbles = 2. So the probability of blue is 2 out of 6, which is the same as 1/3. Red (3 out of 6) is the most likely; green (1 out of 6) is the least likely.

workedExamples.title: Compare two likelihoods
workedExamples.prompt: Which is more likely: drawing a red card from a deck, or drawing a king?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Red cards = 26 out of 52 = 1/2. Kings = 4 out of 52. Since 26 is much greater than 4, drawing a red card is more likely.

workedExamples.title: Use a tally chart
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner with red, blue, green and yellow is spun 40 times: red 14, blue 8, green 9, yellow 9. If the spinner were fair, how many times would each colour be expected, and does the data suggest it is fair?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A fair spinner would give each colour 40 ÷ 4 = 10 spins. Red appeared 14 times, well above 10, while the others were near it. This suggests the spinner may not be completely fair – red seems to come up more often than expected.

workedExamples.title: Judge fairness of a game
workedExamples.prompt: Player 1 wins if a dice shows 1, 2 or 3; Player 2 wins if it shows 4, 5 or 6. Is this game fair?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Player 1 wins with 3 outcomes; Player 2 wins with 3 outcomes. Both have a 3 out of 6 chance, which is 1/2 each. The game is fair.

workedExamples.title: Make a prediction from data
workedExamples.prompt: Over 30 days in April, the weather was sunny 12 days, cloudy 10 days, rainy 8 days. What is the likelihood of a sunny day, and what might you predict for May 1st?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Sunny days = 12 out of 30, which is 2/5 or 40%. Sunny was the most common, so you might predict a sunny day for May 1st. But this is only a sensible guess – weather changes, and one month of data is a small sample.

workedExamples.title: Spot the unfair spinner
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner is half red, one quarter blue and one quarter green. Player 1 wins on red; Player 2 wins on blue or green. Is it fair?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Red covers 1/2 the spinner. Blue and green together cover 1/4 + 1/4 = 1/2. So Player 1 has a 1/2 chance and Player 2 has a 1/2 chance – the game is actually fair, even though it looks lopsided, because the two smaller sections add up to the big one.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 9
workedExamples.prompt: Order these events from least likely to most likely: rolling a 6 on a dice; finding a four-leaf clover; the sun setting tonight; rolling an even number.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 10
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner has 5 equal sections numbered 1 to 5. What is the probability of landing on 3? What is the probability of landing on an even number?
workedExamples.solution: Finding a four-leaf clover, rolling a 6, rolling an even number, the sun setting.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 11
workedExamples.prompt: In a class, 20 like football and 10 like swimming. Is a random child more likely to prefer football or swimming? Give the probability of each.
workedExamples.solution: 3 has a 1 out of 5 chance; even numbers (2 and

workedExamples.title: Guided example 12
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner is spun 40 times with results: red 14, blue 8, green 9, yellow 9. If it were fair, how many of each would you expect? Do you think it is fair? Explain.
workedExamples.solution: 1) A fair spinner gives 40 ÷ 4 = 10 of each; red (14) is well above 10 while the rest are near it, so it may not be fair.

activities.title: Build a Probability Scale
activities.materials: a long strip of paper, a pencil, and a ruler.
steps: Draw a straight line across the paper. Write ‘Impossible’ at the left end and ‘Certain’ at the right end.
Mark ‘Even chance’ exactly in the middle, then ‘Unlikely’ and ‘Likely’ in between.
Think of eight everyday events and write each one on the scale where it belongs.
Show your scale to a family member and ask if they agree with where you placed each event.
Keep your scale – you can use it all through this unit to judge new events.

activities.title: The Great Coin-Flip Experiment
activities.materials: a coin (or a bottle cap) and a tally chart on paper.
steps: Predict how many heads and tails you will get in 50 flips. Write your prediction down.
Flip the coin 50 times, making a tally mark for heads or tails each time.
Count the totals and compare them with your prediction. How close were you?
Now flip another 50 times and add them on. Do the totals get closer to half and half?
This experiment shows why a larger number of trials gives a more reliable picture of a fair coin.

activities.title: Fair or Unfair? The Spinner Test
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a paperclip to spin around a pencil point as a pointer.
steps: Draw a circle split into 4 equal sections coloured red, blue, green and yellow.
Predict how many times each colour will come up in 40 spins.
Spin 40 times and tally the results, then compare with your prediction.
Now draw a second spinner where red fills half the circle. Spin it 40 times and see how the results change.
Talk about which spinner is fair and which is unfair, using probability words.

activities.title: Marble Bag Investigation
activities.materials: a bag or cup and coloured stones or beans – for example 5 red, 3 blue and 2 green.
steps: List all the colours and how many of each, then work out the total.
Predict which colour you are most likely to pull out, and which least likely.
Without looking, pick one stone, record its colour, and put it back. Do this 30 times.
Compare your tally with your prediction. Did red come up most often, as expected?

activities.title: Design a Fair Game (Family Challenge)
activities.materials: a dice or a spinner, and one or more family members to play with.
steps: Invent a two-player game where both players have an equal chance of winning.
Explain to your family why the game is fair, using probability language.
Now change one rule to make the game unfair. Who has the better chance now?
Play both versions and see whether the results feel fair or unfair.

activities.title: Real-World Data Hunt
activities.materials: a notebook and something to observe – cars, birds, or the colour of clothes people wear.
steps: Choose something to observe and decide how you will record it in a tally chart.
Watch and tally for a set time, grouping your marks in fives.
Add up the totals and work out which outcome was most and least common.
Use your data to predict what you would see next, then explain why a bigger sample is more reliable.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand likelihood best? Did any experiment surprise you by not matching your prediction? Why do you think that happened? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: The Five Chance Words Certain = sure to happen (100%). Impossible = cannot happen (0%). Likely = probably will. Unlikely = probably will not. Even chance = 50-50.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: The Probability Scale Runs from Impossible (left) to Certain (right). Even chance sits in the middle. Impossible is 0, even chance is one half, certain is 1.

rules.title: The Probability Rule
rules.text: Probability = number of favourable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. Example: rolling an even number = 3 out of 6 = one half.

terms: How likely an event is to happen
One possible result of an experiment
Equally likely to happen or not (50%)
Every outcome has an equal chance
A chart that counts using grouped marks
A sensible guess about what will happen
commonMistakes: Likely means more than half but not sure
A fair coin need not give exactly 25 of 50
Collect plenty of data before deciding

realProblems.prompt: The forecast gives an 80% chance of rain in Kismaayo. Explain what this means and whether Omar should take a raincoat to the masjid.
realProblems.answer: 1) An 80% chance means rain is very likely (about 8 in 10 similar days), so yes, take a raincoat.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) An 80% chance means rain is very likely (about 8 in 10 similar days), so yes, take a raincoat.

realProblems.prompt: At the Berbera market, a box has 8 dates and 2 sambusas. Farah takes one without looking. Which is she more likely to take, and what is the probability of each?
realProblems.answer: A date is more likely – 8 out of 10 for dates, 2 out of 10 for sambusas.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A date is more likely – 8 out of 10 for dates, 2 out of 10 for sambusas.

realProblems.prompt: Over 30 days, Hani records the weather: sunny 12, cloudy 10, rainy 8. (a) What is the probability of a rainy day? (b) What might you predict for the next day, and why might that prediction be wrong?
realProblems.answer: (a) 8 out of 30 for rain, (b) predict sunny (most common), but weather changes and one month is a small sample.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: (a) 8 out of 30 for rain, (b) predict sunny (most common), but weather changes and one month is a small sample.

realProblems.prompt: A game gives a 1 in 50 chance of winning a toy camel. Describe this chance in words. Would you play many times or just once? Explain.
realProblems.answer: 1 in 50 is unlikely; better to play once, if at all.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 in 50 is unlikely; better to play once, if at all.

realProblems.prompt: Aisha flips a bottle cap 100 times and gets 48 top and 52 bottom. Does this suggest the cap is roughly fair? Explain why a larger number of flips helps.
realProblems.answer: Yes, 48 and 52 are very close to equal, so the cap seems roughly fair; more flips make the result more reliable.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Yes, 48 and 52 are very close to equal, so the cap seems roughly fair; more flips make the result more reliable.

realProblems.answer: 1) A fair spinner gives 40 ÷ 4 = 10 of each; red (14) is well above 10 while the rest are near it, so it may not be fair.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) A fair spinner gives 40 ÷ 4 = 10 of each; red (14) is well above 10 while the rest are near it, so it may not be fair.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A spinner is spun 40 times with results: red 14, blue 8, green 9, yellow 9. If it were fair, how many of each would you expect? Do you think it is fair? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) A fair spinner gives 40 ÷ 4 = 10 of each; red (14) is well above 10 while the rest are near it, so it may not be fair.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: You flip a coin 10 times and get 7 heads and 3 tails. (a) What percentage is heads? (b) Is this proof the coin is unfair? (c) What should you do to test it properly?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: (a) 70% heads, (b) no – 10 flips is too small a sample, (c) flip it many more times (say 100+) and see if heads and tails come out roughly equal.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Design a fair two-player game using a dice, then change one rule to make it unfair, and explain how the chances have changed.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A spinner is 1/2 red, 1/4 blue and 1/4 green. Player 1 wins on red; Player 2 wins on blue or green. Is the game fair? Explain using fractions.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Collecting and Using Data.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: So far we have worked out probability by thinking – this is called theoretical probability. But we can also discover likelihood by collecting data from the real world. Data is information we gather by watching and counting, and a neat way to collect it is with a tally chart. In a tally chart, each time something happens you draw a small mark. To make counting easy, you group the marks in fives: draw four straight marks, then cross them with a fifth mark to make a bundle of five. This means you can count quickly in…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Probability Experiments.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A probability experiment is an activity where we test how likely different outcomes really are. By running experiments we can check whether our predictions match what actually happens. To design a good experiment, follow six clear steps: Make a prediction about the likelihood of each outcome.

selfAssessment: I can use the language of chance – certain, likely, even chance, unlikely, impossible – to describe events.
I can compare the likelihood of two or more different events and put them in order.
I can place events accurately on a probability scale from impossible to certain.
I can list all the possible outcomes of a simple experiment such as flipping a coin or rolling a dice.
I can collect and organise data using tally charts and simple tables.
I can use data to work out likelihood and to make predictions about future events.
I can design and carry out simple probability experiments and record the results carefully.
I can judge whether a game or spinner is fair, using probability to explain your thinking.
